mission.title: Install and Configure Kyverno on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native policy engine that allows platform engineering teams to manage security, compliance, automation, and governance through policy-as-code. Installing Kyverno helps enforce policies in your Kubernetes clusters effectively.

steps.title: Add the Kyverno Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Kyverno Helm repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kyverno using Helm
steps.description:
Install Kyverno in the `kyverno` namespace with the specified version.
```bash
helm install kyverno kyverno/kyverno --version 3.7.1 --namespace kyverno --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Kyverno pods are running successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kyverno
```

steps.title: Configure Kyverno policies
steps.description:
Set up the desired policies by modifying the `values.yaml` file or directly applying policies. Install the Pod Security Standards policies from the official Helm chart:
```bash
helm install kyverno-policies kyverno/kyverno-policies --namespace kyverno
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Kyverno Helm release from your cluster.
```bash
helm uninstall kyverno --namespace kyverno
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) created by Kyverno.
```bash
kubectl delete crd policies.kyverno.io
kubectl delete crd clusterpolicies.kyverno.io
```

uninstall.title: Delete the Kyverno namespace
uninstall.description:
Remove the Kyverno namespace to clean up any remaining resources.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kyverno
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Export the current policies and configurations before upgrading.
```bash
kubectl get policies -A -o yaml > kyverno-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to fetch the latest charts.
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Kyverno
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the Kyverno installation to the latest version.
```bash
helm upgrade kyverno kyverno/kyverno --version 3.7.1 --namespace kyverno
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check if the Kyverno pods are running the new version successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kyverno
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kyverno pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kyverno
```
Common issues include misconfigured policies or resource limits.

troubleshooting.title: Webhook failure
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter webhook failures, verify the webhook configuration:
```bash
kubectl get validatingwebhookconfiguration -n kyverno
```
Ensure that the service is reachable and the correct ports are configured.

troubleshooting.title: Policies not being enforced
troubleshooting.description:
If policies are not being enforced, check the validation failure action settings:
```bash
kubectl get policies -A -o yaml
```
Ensure that the validationFailureAction is set to 'Enforce' where necessary.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kyverno will have all pods running in the `kyverno` namespace, and you will be able to apply and enforce policies on your Kubernetes resources. You can verify the installation by checking the status of the Kyverno pods.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kyverno https://kyverno.github.io/kyverno
helm repo update

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.25.0 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
